explanation:
Java runs on the Java Virtual Machine (JVM), which compiles source code to bytecode (.class files) that runs on any platform with a JVM. This "write once, run anywhere" principle made Java the enterprise standard. The type system is static and strict — every variable must be declared with a type, and the compiler catches type mismatches before runtime.

Java's primitive types (int, double, boolean, char) are not objects, but wrapper classes (Integer, Double, Boolean) provide object equivalents. Autoboxing converts between them automatically. The `String` class is immutable — every modification creates a new String object. `StringBuilder` is used for efficient string concatenation.
key_takeaway: Java is statically typed with primitives and objects — autoboxing bridges them, Strings are immutable, and the JVM makes code platform-independent.
try_it: Write a program that takes a sentence as input, splits it into words, and counts the frequency of each word using a HashMap.

explanation:
Java is a pure object-oriented language — everything (except primitives) is an object, and all code lives inside classes. Encapsulation hides internal state with `private` fields and `public` getters/setters. Inheritance lets subclasses extend parent behavior. Polymorphism allows a parent reference to hold a child object, calling the overridden method.

Abstract classes define partial implementations; interfaces define contracts. Java 8 added default methods to interfaces, blurring the line. The `final` keyword prevents inheritance, method overriding, or variable reassignment.
key_takeaway: Abstract classes for shared state and partial implementation; interfaces for contracts and multiple inheritance. Use `@Override` to prevent subtle bugs from misspelled method names.
try_it: Add a `Triangle` class with three sides. Implement `Comparable<Shape>` to sort shapes by area. Create a list of mixed shapes and sort them.

explanation:
Java's Collections Framework provides data structures for every use case. `ArrayList` is the resizable array (use instead of raw arrays). `LinkedList` is for frequent insertions/deletions. `HashMap` provides O(1) key-value lookups. `HashSet` stores unique elements. `TreeMap` and `TreeSet` maintain sorted order.

The framework uses generics (`List<String>`, `Map<String, Integer>`) for type safety. The `Collections` utility class provides sorting, searching, and shuffling. Streams (Java 8+) enable functional-style operations on collections.
key_takeaway: ArrayList for random access, HashMap for O(1) lookups, TreeMap for sorted keys, HashSet for uniqueness. Use `List.of()` for immutable collections.
try_it: Implement a word frequency counter using `HashMap<String, Integer>`. Read words from a list, count occurrences, then print them sorted by frequency descending using `TreeMap` or `Collections.sort`.

explanation:
Java has checked exceptions (must be declared or caught) and unchecked exceptions (RuntimeExceptions). Checked exceptions enforce error handling at compile time — if a method might throw IOException, callers must handle it. This is controversial: it ensures errors aren't ignored but adds boilerplate.

The try-with-resources statement (Java 7+) automatically closes resources implementing `AutoCloseable`. Custom exceptions extend `Exception` (checked) or `RuntimeException` (unchecked). The `finally` block runs regardless of whether an exception occurred.
key_takeaway: Use try-with-resources for automatic cleanup, catch specific exceptions (not `Exception`), and create custom exceptions for domain-specific error handling.
try_it: Create a `FileProcessor` class that reads a CSV file, parses each line into a `Student` object, and handles malformed lines gracefully with a custom `ParseException`.

explanation:
Java 8 introduced lambda expressions and the Streams API, bringing functional programming to Java. Lambdas are anonymous functions — concise alternatives to anonymous inner classes. Streams process collections declaratively with `filter`, `map`, `reduce`, and `collect` operations, replacing verbose loops.

Streams are lazy — intermediate operations (`filter`, `map`) don't execute until a terminal operation (`collect`, `forEach`, `count`) is called. They can be parallel (`parallelStream()`) for multi-core processing. Method references (`String::length`, `System.out::println`) are shorthand for simple lambdas.
key_takeaway: Streams process collections declaratively — `filter` selects, `map` transforms, `collect` gathers results. Use `Collectors.groupingBy` for SQL-like GROUP BY operations.
try_it: Given a list of transactions (amount, category, date), use streams to find the total spending per category, the largest transaction, and all transactions above the average amount.

explanation:
Java has built-in multithreading — running multiple tasks simultaneously within a single process. The `ExecutorService` manages thread pools to avoid creating/destroying threads. `synchronized` blocks and `Lock` objects prevent race conditions. `CompletableFuture` enables async programming with composable pipelines.

Understanding thread safety is critical: shared mutable state must be synchronized, immutable objects are inherently thread-safe, and `AtomicInteger`/`AtomicReference` provide lock-free thread-safe operations.
key_takeaway: Use `ExecutorService` for thread pools, `AtomicInteger` for lock-free counters, and `CompletableFuture` for composable async pipelines. Always shut down thread pools.
try_it: Implement a parallel web scraper that fetches 10 URLs concurrently using an ExecutorService, collects results into a thread-safe list, and handles timeouts.

explanation:
Build tools automate compilation, testing, packaging, and dependency management. Maven uses XML (`pom.xml`) with a fixed lifecycle (compile, test, package, install). Gradle uses Groovy/Kotlin DSL (`build.gradle`) with a flexible task graph. Both resolve dependencies from Maven Central and manage transitive dependencies.

Modern Java projects prefer Gradle for its flexibility and speed (incremental builds, build cache). Maven remains dominant in enterprise for its predictability. Both support multi-module projects, custom plugins, and CI/CD integration.
example:
```xml
<!-- pom.xml (Maven) -->
<project>
    <modelVersion>4.0.0</modelVersion>
    <groupId>com.example</groupId>
    <artifactId>my-app</artifactId>
    <version>1.0.0</version>
    <packaging>jar</packaging>

    <properties>
        <maven.compiler.source>21</maven.compiler.source>
        <maven.compiler.target>21</maven.compiler.target>
    </properties>

    <dependencies>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>com.google.code.gson</groupId>
            <artifactId>gson</artifactId>
            <version>2.10.1</version>
        </dependency>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.junit.jupiter</groupId>
            <artifactId>junit-jupiter</artifactId>
            <version>5.10.1</version>
            <scope>test</scope>
        </dependency>
    </dependencies>

    <build>
        <plugins>
            <plugin>
                <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
                <artifactId>maven-surefire-plugin</artifactId>
                <version>3.2.3</version>
            </plugin>
        </plugins>
    </build>
</project>
```

```groovy
// build.gradle (Gradle)
plugins {
    id 'java'
    id 'application'
}

java {
    toolchain {
        languageVersion = JavaLanguageVersion.of(21)
    }
}

repositories {
    mavenCentral()
}

dependencies {
    implementation 'com.google.code.gson:gson:2.10.1'
    testImplementation 'org.junit.jupiter:junit-jupiter:5.10.1'
}

tasks.test {
    useJUnitPlatform()
}

application {
    mainClass = 'com.example.Main'
}
```

```bash
# Maven commands
mvn clean compile          # compile
mvn test                   # run tests
mvn package                # create JAR
mvn dependency:tree        # show dependency tree

# Gradle commands
gradle build               # compile + test + package
gradle test                # run tests
gradle run                 # run application
gradle dependencies        # show dependency tree
```
key_takeaway: Maven for predictable enterprise builds, Gradle for flexible modern builds. Both manage dependencies, run tests, and package JARs from the command line.
try_it: Create a Gradle project with JUnit 5 and Gson. Write a test that deserializes a JSON string into a Java record, then run `gradle test` to verify.

explanation:
Java interview problems test your understanding of OOP principles, collections framework, and concurrency. The most common patterns involve HashMap-based lookups, StringBuilder for string manipulation, and linked list pointer operations. Java's strong typing means you need explicit type handling — generics, autoboxing, and null safety are frequent interview topics.

Beyond algorithms, Java interviews often test design patterns (Builder, Factory, Observer), exception handling best practices, and stream API usage. The Collections framework (ArrayList, HashMap, TreeMap, PriorityQueue) is the foundation — know their time complexities and when to use each. For concurrency, understand synchronized, volatile, and the java.util.concurrent package.
key_takeaway: Master HashMap and StringBuilder — they solve most Java interview problems. Know Collections time complexities by heart.
try_it: Try solving Two Sum with the HashMap approach, then attempt it with a sorted array using two pointers.

practice_challenges.title: Two Sum
practice_challenges.hint: Use a HashMap<Integer, Integer> to store value-to-index. For each number, check if (target - num) exists.
practice_challenges.description: Given an array of integers and a target, return indices of two numbers that add up to the target.

practice_challenges.title: Reverse Linked List
practice_challenges.hint: Use three pointers: prev, current, next. At each step, reverse the current node's pointer and advance all three.
practice_challenges.description: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list and return the new head.

practice_challenges.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
practice_challenges.hint: Use a HashSet<Character> and two pointers. Expand right, shrink left when duplicate found. Track max length.
practice_challenges.description: Given a string, find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.

practice_challenges.title: LRU Cache
practice_challenges.hint: Extend LinkedHashMap or implement a custom doubly linked list with a HashMap for O(1) get/put.
practice_challenges.description: Design an LRU cache supporting get and put in O(1) time with a fixed capacity.
practice_challenges.example_input: LRUCache(2); put(1,1); put(2,2); get(1); put(3,3); get(2)

practice_challenges.title: Merge k Sorted Lists
practice_challenges.hint: Add the head of each list to a PriorityQueue<ListNode> sorted by value. Poll smallest, add its next to the queue.
practice_challenges.description: Given k sorted linked lists, merge them into one sorted linked list using a min-heap.
